title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Cacao's Amazonian origin
description: Upper Amazon basin (Ecuador/Peru) — cacao evolved here; modern DNA analysis points to this region as the true origin of Theobroma cacao (Zarrillo 2018).

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Cacao's Amazonian origin
description: Olmec Mesoamerica (Veracruz) — earliest documented cacao consumption ~1500 BCE.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Cacao's Amazonian origin
description: Maya lowlands (Guatemala/Belize) — cacao buried with kings; depicted in the Popol Vuh.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Cacao's Amazonian origin
description: Tenochtitlan (Mexico City) — Aztec capital where cacao served as currency and sacred drink ('xocolatl').

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Spanish conquest transplant
description: Tenochtitlan 1519 — Cortés encounters Moctezuma drinking xocolatl. Cacao enters Spanish colonial economy via pochteca trade networks.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Spanish conquest transplant
description: Madrid — Spanish court develops sweetened chocolate; monopoly on recipe lasts ~100 years before spreading to Italy and France.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Spanish conquest transplant
description: Rome / Italy — chocolate spreads via Jesuit and diplomatic networks in the 17th century.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Spanish conquest transplant
description: Paris — chocolate arrives via Anne of Austria's Spanish court marriage to Louis XIII (1615).

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Spanish conquest transplant
description: London — first chocolate house opens 1657; establishment gathering spots for elite.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Colonial plantation spread
description: Caribbean + South America — Spanish + Portuguese establish plantations across Venezuela, Trinidad, Bahia Brazil using enslaved African labour.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Colonial plantation spread
description: Bahia Brazil — 17th-century plantation economy.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Colonial plantation spread
description: Trinidad — origin of the Trinitario hybrid (Criollo × Forastero).

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Colonial plantation spread
description: Venezuela — Criollo cultivation; still produces the rarest cacao variety today.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: African transplant (late 19th century)
description: São Tomé and Príncipe — Portuguese transplant cacao from Brazil to West Africa 1822; become staging base for continental spread.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: African transplant (late 19th century)
description: Côte d'Ivoire — becomes world's largest producer by late 20th century (42% of world cacao today).

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: African transplant (late 19th century)
description: Ghana — British colonial introduction 1879; today produces ~17% of world cacao.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: African transplant (late 19th century)
description: Nigeria — 7% of world production today.

title: LAYER 2 — Language Diffusion: xocolatl → chocolate
description: Nahuatl (Aztec) — 'xocolatl' meaning 'bitter water'.

title: LAYER 2 — Language Diffusion: xocolatl → chocolate
description: Spanish — 'chocolate'. Sweetened with cane sugar to mask the bitterness.

title: LAYER 2 — Language Diffusion: xocolatl → chocolate
description: French — 'chocolat'.

title: LAYER 2 — Language Diffusion: xocolatl → chocolate
description: German — 'Schokolade'.

title: LAYER 2 — Language Diffusion: xocolatl → chocolate
description: English — 'chocolate'. The Nahuatl word survives in every European language despite genocide of Aztec speakers.

title: LAYER 3 — Modern Supply Chain (today)
description: Côte d'Ivoire — 42% of world cacao. Child labour in the industry is a documented ongoing crisis (US Dept of Labor 2020).

title: LAYER 3 — Modern Supply Chain (today)
description: Ghana — 17% of world cacao.

title: LAYER 3 — Modern Supply Chain (today)
description: Indonesia — 13% of world cacao.

title: LAYER 3 — Modern Supply Chain (today)
description: Nigeria — 7% of world cacao.

title: LAYER 3 — Modern Supply Chain (today)
description: Amsterdam — world's largest cocoa port.

title: LAYER 3 — Modern Supply Chain (today)
description: Germany — largest chocolate-exporting manufacturer in the EU.

title: LAYER 3 — Modern Supply Chain (today)
description: USA Northeast (Hershey PA) — historical and modern American chocolate manufacturing hub.
